title: Focal dystonia: CNS-driven, muscle fiber hypertrophy
vignette: A mouse model of focal dystonia due to mutations in DYT-TOR1A is being studied. Humans with this mutation have variable expressivity of diverse forms of dystonia, including cervical dystonia (ie, torticollis), writer's cramp, and lower limb dystonias. Histologic analysis of an affected muscle in one of the experimental mice is most likely to show which of the following?

clues.text: focal dystonia due to mutations in DYT-TOR1A
clues.note: Dystonia is a disorder of abnormal CNS NEURONAL SIGNALING (basal ganglia/motor pathways), not a primary muscle disease. The muscle itself is structurally near-normal; the problem is sustained involuntary contraction commanded by the brain.

Focal dystonias like writer's cramp involve sustained, repetitive involuntary contractions of a specific muscle group. This chronic increased workload acts like exercise on the muscle, causing the fibers to HYPERTROPHY rather than degenerate.

Because the lesion is neural, biopsy of the overworked muscle shows either normal histology or HYPERTROPHY of muscle fibers from the repetitive contractions — there is no inflammation, necrosis, or storage abnormality.

whatMatters: Dystonia originates in the CNS (abnormal neural signaling), so the muscle is not primarily diseased. Chronic involuntary contraction works the muscle hard, producing muscle FIBER HYPERTROPHY (the same response as exercise). Answer D.
strategy: When a 'muscle biopsy' question stems from a NEUROLOGIC/CNS disorder (dystonia, spasticity), expect normal muscle or work-induced hypertrophy — not inflammation/necrosis/storage. Map each distractor to the specific muscle disease it actually fingerprints (storage, dystrophy, myositis, rhabdo).

eliminations.label: enlarged lysosomes — Pompe/storage disease
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: enlarged, glycogen-filled lysosomes are seen in Pompe disease (acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency) and other lysosomal storage myopathies. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: dystonia isn't a storage disorder; the muscle has no lysosomal accumulation. ✅ Correct if: the case were an infantile/metabolic myopathy with cardiomegaly and hypotonia (Pompe).

eliminations.label: fibrofatty replacement — muscular dystrophy
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: replacement of muscle by fibrofatty tissue is characteristic of muscular dystrophies (e.g., Duchenne). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: dystonia doesn't destroy and replace fibers; it's a neural signaling problem, not a degenerative dystrophy. ✅ Correct if: the case were a dystrophinopathy with progressive weakness and pseudohypertrophy.

eliminations.label: inflammatory infiltrate — myositis
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: inflammatory cell infiltrates define the inflammatory myopathies (polymyositis, dermatomyositis, inclusion-body myositis). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: dystonia is non-inflammatory; there's no autoimmune muscle attack. ✅ Correct if: the case had proximal weakness with elevated CK and an autoimmune inflammatory myopathy.

eliminations.label: necrosis without inflammation — rhabdomyolysis/toxic myopathy
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: myofiber necrosis without much inflammation suggests rhabdomyolysis or a toxic/necrotizing myopathy (e.g., statin-associated). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: dystonic muscle isn't undergoing necrosis; the contractions cause hypertrophy, not cell death. ✅ Correct if: the case were rhabdomyolysis (myoglobinuria, very high CK) or a necrotizing myopathy.

reasoning: The muscle itself is not primarily diseased
Chronic involuntary contraction overworks the muscle (like exercise)
Result = muscle fiber hypertrophy → answer D
mechanism: Dystonia is a movement disorder of sustained, involuntary muscle contractions producing abnormal postures; it arises from abnormal central neural signaling (basal ganglia, motor cortex, supplementary motor areas), with genetic forms such as DYT-TOR1A (torsinA). Because the defect is neural, the affected muscle is histologically near-normal or shows fiber HYPERTROPHY from repetitive contraction — analogous to training-induced hypertrophy — without inflammation, necrosis, storage material, or fibrofatty replacement.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neuro > Movement disorders/dystonia: CNS origin (basal ganglia), focal dystonias (cervical/torticollis, blepharospasm, writer's cramp), genetic (DYT-TOR1A); muscle is normal or hypertrophied. Contrast muscle-biopsy fingerprints: storage (Pompe), dystrophy (fibrofatty), myositis (inflammation), rhabdo (necrosis).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Key insight: dystonia = a BRAIN problem, so the muscle isn't sick. Overuse → hypertrophy. Don't pick a primary myopathy finding.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on dystonia, torticollis, hypertrophy vs atrophy, fibrofatty replacement, myositis, rhabdomyolysis? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Each wrong option is a real muscle-disease histology (Pompe, dystrophy, myositis, rhabdo). The trap is treating dystonia as a muscle disease — it's neural, so the muscle just hypertrophies.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Right answer (D). One-liner: CNS movement disorder → overworked but otherwise normal muscle → fiber hypertrophy.

keywords.back: Normal or fiber hypertrophy (CNS disorder, not a myopathy)
Abnormal CNS neural signaling (basal ganglia/motor pathways)

memoryHook: Dystonia is a BRAIN command problem — the muscle just gets a workout and bulks up (hypertrophy), it isn't diseased.
